coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of diethylenetriamine-modified carbon quantum dots for dual sensing Fe3+ and Co2+and its application
coredata.dc:creator: Pu, Zixuan
Wang, Qian
Li, Kuan
Fan, Wenlin
Li, Mingtian
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification is an important method for regulating the carbon quantum dots structure and enhancing the performance. Herein, green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) were synthesized by the solvothermal method using p-phenylenediamine as carbon source, diethylenetriamine as modifier and anhydrous ethanol as solvent. G-CQDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and Zeta potential. The particle size of G-CQDs was about 2.11 nm and the fluorescence quantum yield of G-CQDs was increased from 1.07 % to 28.90 % after diethylenetriamine modification. The increase of nitrogen and oxygen contents after modification enriches the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface, providing more reaction sites for metal ions. It was further found that G-CQDs can detect Fe3+ and Co2+ based on two different fluorescence “on-off” mechanisms with detection limits of 0.88 μM and 0.95 μM, respectively. In practical applications, the excellent fluorescence performance of G-CQDs for information anti-counterfeiting has a good effect. It was used for the detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in tap water, the spike recovery were in the range of 98.11 % ∼ 106.80 % and 95.87 % ∼ 104.49 %, respectively, showing good accuracy and reproducibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Diethylenetriamine
Fe3+
Co2+
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of metal ions on fluorescence
Response of G-CQDs to Fe3+ and Co2+
Quenching mechanism of G-CQDs by Fe3+ and Co2+
Detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Innovation Fund of Postgraduate, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering (No. Y2023004 and CX2023011 ), for which all the authors are grateful.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A ∼ B) TEM and particle size distribution of G-CQDs; (C ∼ D) High-resolution TEM images of G-CQDs.
FTIR spectra of x-CQDs and G-CQDs.
XPS surveys of x-CQDs (A) and G-CQDs (B).
Narrow-scan XPS spectra of G-CQDs (A) C1s; (B) N1s; (C) O1s.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of G-CQDs (A) and x-CQDs (C); fluorescence spectra of G-CQDs (B) and x-CQDs (D).
Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of G-CQDs (A) and x-CQDs (B).
Fluorescence spectra (A) and bar diagram (B) of the effect of metal ions on G-CQDs fluorescence.
Effects of different concentrations of Fe3+ and Co2+ on the fluorescence of G-CQDs (A, C) fluorescence spectra; (B, D) Linear graph.
Effects of amino acids on fluorescence of G-CQDs (A, B).

Effects of amino acids on fluorescence of G-CQDs (A) Fluorescence spectra of the influence of L-Cys on G-CQDs-Fe3+; (B) Photos of G-CQDs (left), G-CQDs-Fe3+ (middle), G-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Cys (right) solution in daylight; C) Fluorescence spectra of the influence of L-Cys on G-CQDs-Co2+; D) Photos of G-CQDs (left), G-CQDs-Co2+ (middle), G-CQDs-Co2+-L-Cys (right) solution in daylight.
(A) Zeta potentials of G-CQDs、G-CQDs-Fe3+ and G-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Cys; (B) Zeta potentials of G-CQDs、G-CQDs-Co2+ and G-CQDs-Co2+-L-Cys.
Time-resolved PL decay of G-CQDs (A); G-CQDs-Fe3+ (B); G-CQDs-Co2+ (C).
Fluorescence spectras (A) and bar charts (B) of the effects of different anions; organic solvents (C) and water content (D) on G-CQDs; bar charts of the effect of temperature on G-CQDs(E).
Pictures taken in daylight (top) and under 365 nm UV light (bottom).
Comparison between the proposed method and other techniques for Fe3+ detection.
Comparison between the proposed method and other techniques for Co2+ detection.
G-CQDs spike recovery test for Fe3+ and Co2+ in tap water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of diethylenetriamine-modified carbon quantum dots for dual sensing Fe3+ and Co2+and its application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong, 643000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong, 643000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Zigong, 643000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Zigong, 643000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong, 643000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface modification is an important method for regulating the carbon quantum dots structure and enhancing the performance. Herein, green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) were synthesized by the solvothermal method using p-phenylenediamine as carbon source, diethylenetriamine as modifier and anhydrous ethanol as solvent. G-CQDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and Zeta potential. The particle size of G-CQDs was about 2.11 nm and the fluorescence quantum yield of G-CQDs was increased from 1.07 % to 28.90 % after diethylenetriamine modification. The increase of nitrogen and oxygen contents after modification enriches the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface, providing more reaction sites for metal ions. It was further found that G-CQDs can detect Fe3+ and Co2+ based on two different fluorescence “on-off” mechanisms with detection limits of 0.88 μM and 0.95 μM, respectively. In practical applications, the excellent fluorescence performance of G-CQDs for information anti-counterfeiting has a good effect. It was used for the detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in tap water, the spike recovery were in the range of 98.11 % ∼ 106.80 % and 95.87 % ∼ 104.49 %, respectively, showing good accuracy and reproducibility.

Solvothermal synthesis of green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) and detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ using two different fluorescence “on-off” mechanisms.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is one of the essential trace elements in human physiological processes and plays a vital role in oxygen uptake, oxygen metabolism and electron transfer [1]. Both insufficient and excessive levels of iron in the body can cause physical diseases such as anemia, cancer, mental decline, Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease [2–4]. Cobalt ions (Co2+) have the ability to regulate enzyme and cofactor catalytic activity, promote the synthesis of red blood cells and certain enzymes. Long-term exposure to high concentrations of Co2+ can cause diseases such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and damage to the nervous system [5,6]. In addition, excessive emission of Fe3+ and Co2+ in industrial wastewater can cause water pollution, resulting in deteriorated water quality and toxicity to the environment, aquatic life, and vegetation. Hence, monitoring and controlling the concentrations of metal ions such as Fe3+ and Co2+ in industrial wastewater is an important aspect of environmental protection and sustainable development. Currently, atomic absorption spectrometry [7], surface plasmon resonance [8], colorimetric method [9], and fluorescence method [10] are mainly used for the detection of metal ion concentrations. However, most of the methods are not conducive to wide application due to disadvantages such as expensive equipment, high cost and complicated sample preparation. Fluorescence method is widely used due to its advantages such as technical simplicity, excellent sensitivity and low cost.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm and quasi-spherical shape [4]. CQDs can be synthesized using various methods, including hydrothermal/solvothermal [11,12],electrochemical [13], chemical oxidation [14], microwave [15], and so on. Among them, hydrothermal/solvothermal methods are widely used for CQDs synthesis due to the simplicity of operation and the ability to perform surface modifications and element doping during the preparation process [11]. Compared with conventional quantum dots, CQDs has the advantages of abundant sources of raw materials, easy preparation process, and eco-friendliness [16,17]. Due to their excellent biocompatibility, small size, low toxicity, tunable fluorescence emission, as well as easily modifiable surface properties [18,19], CQDs have been widely applied in various fields, such as anti-counterfeiting [20], bioimaging [21], optoelectronic devices [22], drug analysis [23], wastewater detection [24], and other fields.

In conventional preparation methods, CQDs typically exhibit poor fluorescence performance and lower fluorescence quantum yields. Therefore, the surface modification and functionalization of CQDs are crucial [25]. Amine molecular organic compounds (such as ethylenediamine, thiourea, etc.) are common passivators and precursors. Their addition enhances the photoluminescence performance while boosting the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs [26]. Amine molecules are also excellent doping sources, which can not only modify the defects on the surface of CQDs, but also provide a large number of sites for the metal cations, further improving the bonding ability of the metal cations with CQDs, thus achieving the effect of sensitive detection of metal ions [27]. The biocompatible CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using alanine as carbon source and ethylenediamine as surface passivating agent. The CQDs were capable of effectively labeling the cell membrane and nucleus of MCF-7 cells, as well as detecting nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) with high sensitivity, achieving a detection limit as low as 25.1 nM [28]. Chaghaghazard et al. dissolved chitosan, citric acid, and thiourea in DDW (double-distilled water) and synthesized CQDs using a hydrothermal method. The CQDs exhibited ultra-sensitive detection of Hg2+ within a wide linear range. The successful detection of Hg2+ in actual lake water samples also indicates their significant potential for environmental analysis applications [29]. The application of CQDs in the detection of metal ions is already widespread. However, there are relatively few reports on the simultaneous detection of two metal ions using a single type of CQDs. This is because it is challenging to find a material that can mask the effect of one metal ion. Therefore, the development of a new type of dual-responsive fluorescent probe for metal ions is highly interesting.

In this paper, a novel kind of green fluorescent CQDs (G-CQDs) that can simultaneously detect Fe3+ and Co2+ were prepared using p-phenylenediamine as carbon source and diethylenetriamine as modifier, and a new metal ion dual-response fluorescent probe was developed and applied to anti-counterfeiting and testing of actual water samples by using its excellent fluorescence performance and fluorescence stability.

P-phenylenediamine, quinine sulfate, Rhodamine B, ethylene glycol, pentanediol, triethanolamine, amino acids (L-Arg, L-Cys, L-His, L-Lys, L-Val), metallic salts (MgCl2, LiCl, NiCl2, SnCl2, BaCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, PbCl2, KCl, CuCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, MnCl2, CeCl3, HgCl2, AgNO3, Co(NO3)2, La(NO3)3 were purchased from Mcklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Diethylenetriamine was purchased from Chengdu West Asia Chemical Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). The solvents of anhydrous ethanol (EA), dichloromethane (DCM), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol (MT), n-hexane (HEX), ethyl acetate (EAC), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethylene glycol (EG), n-butanol (NBA) were bought from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). The concentrated sulfuric acid was purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd (Chongqing, China). Ultrapure water was used for all solution preparations throughout the experiments. All reagents were analytically pure and did not require further purification.

G-CQDs were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal process. Specifically, 0.2 g of p-phenylenediamine and 50 μL of diethylenetriamine were dissolved in 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 15 min, and then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor. After heated at 180 °C about 10 h, the reactor was cooled to room temperature, and then the mixture was filtered, centrifuged to remove insoluble materials and larger particles. The brown solution was dialyzed in ultrapure water with a dialysis membrane (500 Da) for 48 ∼ 72 h. The dialyzed solution was lyophilized under vacuum at ‒ 60 °C for 48 h to obtain powder (G-CQDs). The working solution of G-CQDs with 0.5 mg/L was prepared by the powder dissolved in anhydrous ethanol for the subsequent experiments. x-CQDs were synthesized using the same procedures as G-CQDs, but without the addition of diethylenetriamine.

The particle size and morphology were observed by JEM2100 PLUS transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HTEM). The chemical composition, element valence and surface structure of G-CQDs was characterized using Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Optical properties are measured by UV-PC1800 UV–Visible spectrophotometer and F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The potential change and fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-CQDs was further explored by using Zetasizer Nano ZSE nano particle potential analyzer. The fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was examined by Edinburgh FLS1000 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer.

The fluorescence quantum yields of G-CQDs and x-CQDs were determined by the reference method. The fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate (QS) dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 is known to be 54 %, and that of Rhodamine B (RhB) dissolved in anhydrous ethanol is 92 % [30,31]. Therefore, the fluorescence amount of G-CQDs was determined using QS as a reference, and the fluorescence quantum yield of x-CQDs was determined using RhB as a reference. The fluorescence quantum yield (φ X) was calculated according to the following equation [32]:
φ X
=
φ ST
(
k X
k
S T
)
(
Α X
Α
S T
)
Where φ X and φ ST represents the fluorescence quantum yields of the experimental sample and the standard sample, respectively. k X and k ST are the linear slope values of the absorbance of the experimental and standard samples, respectively, with respect to the integrated area of the corresponding fluorescence peaks. A X and A ST are the refractive indices of the solvents of the experimental and standard samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ions on fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: At room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the working solution was used as the control group. Nineteen metal ion solutions (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, La3+, Li+, Ag+, Zn2+, Co2+, Na+, Mn2+, K+, Ni2+, Sn2+) were added to the working solution (2 mL), and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The concentrations of the metal ion solutions were all 5.0 mM, and the addition volume was 20 μL.
The fluorescence stability of G-CQDs in anionic species, solution acidity and alkalinity, water content, organic solvent and temperature was investigated using fluorescence spectrophotometer.

With reference to the traditional ink formulation, the working solution, ethylene glycol, pentanediol, and triethanolamine were blended in the proportion of 64 %, 20 %, 13 %, and 3 % by volume, respectively, and then the components were dispersed homogeneously to obtain green fluorescent ink.
Appropriate amount of tap water was taken as the actual sample, centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min, the supernatant was extracted and then filtered with 0.22 μm filter paper and prepared for use.

The surface topography, particle size distribution, chemical structure as well as fluorescent properties of G-CQDs and x-CQDs were investigated by TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–Vis, fluorescence sprctra and Zeta potential.
As shown in Fig. 1 , G-CQDs was found to have quasi-spherical structure and uniform in size, with an average crystallite size of 2.11 nm ± 0.32 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2 (B), G-CQDs show a broad absorption band in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1, which includes a number of weak absorption peaks, which mainly corresponds to the N–H/O–H telescoping vibrations [33]. The weak absorption peak at 2349 cm−1 and the sharp peaks at 1612 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1 are generated by the C N, CO, and CC respectively the stretching vibrations [34,35]. The weak peaks at 1260 cm−1 and 1126 cm−1 are considered to be the stretching vibrations of N–H and CO groups [36]. The strong peaks at 829 cm−1 and 716 cm−1 are attributed to the characteristic absorption of aromatic azircones [37].

The infrared spectrum of x-CQDs is similar to that of G-CQDs, except that the absorption peaks intensities of x-CQDs are weaker compared with those of G-CQDs. The peaks at 1510 cm−1, 1256 cm−1, and 832 cm−1 are remarkably significant, which indicates that the addition of diethylenetriamine enriches the nitrogenous and oxygenous groups on surface of G-CQDs, such as amino groups, carboxyl groups and oxygen groups.

In order to further investigate the elemental composition, surface structure and functional groups of G-CQDs, x-CQDs and G-CQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen from the full spectra analysis of XPS of x-CQDs and G-CQDs, that the nitrogen content of G-CQDs increased by 1.69 % compared with that of x-CQDs, indicating that diethylenetriamine was successfully doped into G-CQDs. Fig. 4 (A) showed the C1s spectrum of G-CQDs with three characteristic peaks at 284.28 eV, 285.38 eV and 287.48 eV from CC/C–C, C–N/C–O and CO, respectively [36]. The N1s spectrum of G-CQDs was shown in Fig. 4 (B), with three peaks at 398.58 eV, 399.68 eV and 400.78 eV are be assigned to CN, N–H and C–N–C groups, respectively [38]. Two peaks at 530.78 eV and 532.68 eV in the O1s spectrum of Fig. 4 (C) are corresponded to C–O and CO groups, respectively [39]. The presence of functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups enhances the dispersion of G-CQDs in solution, while the large number of defects generated by these functional groups will provide active sites for metal ion detection.

The x-CQDs and G-CQDs optical properties were examined using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 5 (A) showed that there are three obvious absorption peaks of G-CQDs at about 205 nm, 390 nm and 540 nm, which are attributed to the solvent effect of G-CQDs sample, the conjugated system on surface of G-CQDs and the macromolecular structure within G-CQDs, respectively [39]. When exposed to external radiation, the electrons will jump from the ground state to the excited state, generating n → π* electron leap activity [40]. However, in order to bring the system to a steady state, the electrons will fall back from the excited state to the ground state, and the process of falling back produces secondary radiation from the electrons, which manifests itself in the form of fluorescence.

As shown in Fig. 5(B), changing the excitation wavelength in the range of 370 ∼ 450 nm, although the maximum emission wavelength intensity of G-CQDs first increases and then decreases (reaching a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm), the emission peak position is located at about 500 nm, which is in the range of 500 nm of green light being consistent with the color is seen by the naked eye in the inset of Fig. 5(A). Under 390 nm excitation, the strongest radiation is produced by electrons falling back from the excited state to the ground state, and the same radiation is still produced under the other excitation wavelengths, but the intensity is weakened. The above phenomenon indicates that the surface of G-CQDs possesses numerous defects caused by many reactive functional groups, and such defects can act as excitation energy traps thus causing G-CQDs to emit green fluorescence.

Fig. 5(C) showed that the absorption peaks of x-CQDs at both 242 nm and 308 nm are attributed to n → π* transitions of C–C and C–N bonds, respectively, and there is a broad absorption between 400 nm and 600 nm, which corresponds to n → π* transition from the bonds of C–O or C–N [41,42]. It can be seen from Fig. 5(D), the intensity of the maximum emission wavelength is first enhanced and then weakened with the increase of the excitation wavelength from 470 nm to 540 nm, and the strongest is at the excitation wavelength of 510 nm. The emission peak is centered around 610 nm, and about 2/3 of the fluorescence spectrum is in the orange range (597 ∼ 622 nm), 1/3 is in the red range (622 ∼ 760 nm), thus x-CQDs exhibit a pale pink light (Fig. 5(C) inset). Compared to x-CQDs, the property of G-CQDs with fluorescence emission peak that hardly changes with the variation of excitation wavelength is more advantageous for subsequent detection.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the linear fit lines of QS and G-CQDs were plotted with the integrated area of fluorescence emission peaks as the vertical coordinate and the corresponding absorbance magnitude as the horizontal coordinate, respectively. According to the formula:
φ X
=
φ ST
(
k X
k
S T
)
(
Α X
Α
S T
)

The fluorescence quantum yield of G-CQDs was calculated to be 28.9 %. x-CQDs were determined and calculated in the same way as G-CQDs, except that the reference substance was changed to RhB, and the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 1.07 %, which means the fluorescence quantum yield of x-CQDs was dramatically increased after modified, providing a greater advantage for subsequent detection. Macroscopically, this is also corroborated with the insets in Fig. 5(A) and (C), where the brightness of the pink fluorescence of x-CQDs is significantly lower than that of the green fluorescence of G-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of G-CQDs to Fe3+ and Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: The results from FTIR and XPS have been validated to confirm that G-CQDs consist of several surface functional groups (amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl). The fluorescence of G-CQDs can be enhanced or quenched by the interaction of the functional groups with metal ions. As shown in Fig. 7 (A ∼ B), Fe3+ and Co2+ had the greatest impact on quenching fluorescence of G-CQDs, while the fluorescence intensity of other metal ions remained above 80 %, which suggests that G-CQDs can specifically recognize Fe3+ and Co2+. An interesting phenomenon is that the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs decreases with the addition of Fe3+ and the emission peak position is significantly red-shifted, while Co2+ only decreases the intensity without changing the position. These results indicated that G-CQDs could be employed as fluorescent “on-off” probes to detect Fe3+ and Co2+.

As shown in Fig. 8 (A), the emission peak width of G-CQDs gradually broadens with increasing Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 112.5 μM, and the emission peaks of G-CQDs were gradually red-shifted from 500 nm to 550 nm. Two sets of linear relationships were observed between the Fe3+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs at different concentration ranges in Fig. 8(B). The linear regression equation for Fe3+ concentration between 0 ∼ 50 μM was Y = ‒ 0.0146X + 0.9769 (R 2 = 0.9820). The linear regression equation for Fe3+ concentrations between 50 ∼ 112.5 μM was Y = ‒ 0.0024X + 0.3829 (R 2 = 0.9352). According to the triple standard deviation principle, the limit of detection (LOD) can be calculated by LOD = 3σ/k. Where σ is the standard deviation of LOD (σ = 0.0007) and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of G-CQDs for Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.88 μM. Compared to other carbon quantum dots (Table 1 ), G-CQDs have excellent sensitivity in detecting Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of G-CQDs by Fe3+ and Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: In previous report, the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by Fe3+ was attributed to intramolecular dynamics [13,44]. CQDs are excited by energetic light to produce excited state electrons, and when Fe3+ is added to them, such electrons fill as many empty 3d orbitals in the outer layer of Fe3+ as possible [51], ultimately forming unstable, non-radiative complexes, which show fluorescence as “turn off”. The introduction of Co2+ causes CQDs fluorescence to occur as a static quench. The amine groups present on the surface of the fluorescent nanoparticles act as multiple ligands, which coordinate with Co2+ ions to form non-fluorescent complexes, which in turn block the channels of electrons falling back, thus the luminescence is quenched [48]. To validate this theory, the amino acids were utilized to restore the fluorescence of the G-CQDs-Fe3+ and G-CQDs-Co2+ quench systems. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence of the working solution were slightly quenched by the five amino acids (L-Cys, L-His, L-Arg, L-Val, L-Lys), but the fluorescence intensity still is above 85 %. This indicates that the restoration of the fluorescence of the quenched system is not directly caused by amino acids.

It can be seen from Fig. 10 (A) , the fluorescence of the G-CQDs-Fe3+ system remained essentially unchanged for the first 20 min after the addition of L-Cys. After 20 min, the state of the G-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Cys system undergoes a sudden change, with fluorescence enhancement and the broad emission peak around 550 nm gradually splitting into two distinct emission peaks. As shown in Fig. 10 (B), the leftmost cuvette is the G-CQDs working solution, the center is G-CQDs-Fe3+, and the rightmost is a photograph taken 30 min after the addition of L-Cys to the G-CQDs-Fe3+ solution. What is clear is that the first two solution systems are more stable and do not produce any precipitation or noticeable changes over time. After addition of L-Cys, the G-CQDs-Fe3+ system became extremely unstable, and a yellow precipitate was produced after 30 min of standing without other operations. This macroscopic phenomenon of the solution strongly explains the reason for the fluorescence spectrum change. Upon addition of L-Cys, the amino and carboxyl groups will chelate with Fe3+ to form more stable chelating compounds, extruding the electrons of G-CQDs from the 3d empty orbitals of Fe3+. The extruded G-CQDs electrons are reconverted to excited state electrons and then fall back to the ground state to emit fluorescence, and then the fluorescence of the system is gradually restored with the intensification of the chelation reaction and the increase of excited state electrons.

As shown in Fig. 10(C) and (D), the addition of L-Cys hardly changed the fluorescence of the G-CQDs-Co2+ system, which is similar to that of the G-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Cys system. The probable reason is that Co2+ were directly coordinated by O or N atoms from the functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc.) on the surface of G-CQDs to block the luminescence channels, and the G-CQDs-Co2+ complex formed is extremely stable and difficult to be broken.

In order to further verify the probable reason, the zeta potentials of several fluorescent systems were measured, including G-CQDs, G-CQDs-Fe3+, G-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Cys, G-CQDs-Co2+, and G-CQDs-Co2+-L-Cys. As shown in Fig. 11 (A), a large number of positive charges existed on the surface of G-CQDs, and the zeta potential was 22.90 mV, and the addition of Fe3+ increased the positive charge to 25.35 mV. This is due to the effect of intramolecular dynamics, the electrons of G-CQDs will occupy the empty orbitals in the outer layer of Fe3+ so that the increase in the number of positive charges in the solution leads to fluorescence “turn off” [52]. However, the potential of the G-CQDs-Fe3+ system decreased significantly from 25.35 mV to 17.85 mV after L-Cys was added. It can be explained that the introduction of L-Cys deprives Fe3+ from the surface of G-CQDs to form a more stable chelating compound, and it precipitates. Moreover, the compound is uncharged and can absorb part of the positive charge in the G-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Cys fluorescence system, so that the fluorescence can be “turned on” [53].

As shown in Fig. 11(B), the potential of the fluorescence system decreased from 22.90 mV to 20.88 mV after the addition of Co2+ to the G-CQDs solution, which was opposite to the change after addition Fe3+. While the potential did not change significantly after L-Cys was added to the G-CQDs-Co2+ system, and the decline was only 0.45 mV from 20.88 mV to 20.43 mV, indicating that the complexation between Co2+ and G-CQDs was very stable, and the introduction of L-Cys could not break the static quench mechanism, and less positive charge was absorbed on the surface of G-CQDs leading the zeta potential decline. In summary, the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching of G-CQDs by Fe3+ and Co2+ are different, so G-CQDs can realize the simultaneous detection of two metal ions.

The changes of fluorescence lifetime before and after G-CQDs dropping Fe3+ and Co2+ contribute to the understanding of fluorescence quenching mechanism. Fig. 12 shows the fluorescence decay trajectories of the three systems of G-CQDs, G-CQDs-Fe3+, and G-CQDs-Co2+. It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQD was shortened from 4.153 ns to 2.149 ns by the addition of Fe3+. This is due to the collision of G-CQDs with Fe3+, which produces an energy- or charge-transfer effects, leading to fluorescence quenching and drastically shortening the fluorescence lifetime [54]. However, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQD did not change significantly with the addition of Co2+, which shortened from 4.153 ns to 4.114 ns. This is due to the interaction of G-CQD with Co2+ to form non-fluorescent or weak fluorescent substances, resulting in a small change in the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQD [13]. The change of fluorescence lifetime indicates that the former belongs to dynamic quenching and the latter is static quenching, which further confirms the previous results.

The effects of 11 anions (H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, Cl−, ClO4 −, NO2 −, NO3 −, OH−, SiO3 2−, SCN−, SO4 2−) on the fluorescence of G-CQDs are shown in Fig. 13 (A and B). The anions of H2PO4 − react with the carboxyl groups on the surface of G-CQDs to form phosphate compounds, which cause a certain degree of quench in the fluorescence of G-CQDs. The addition of all other anions could maintain the fluorescence of G-CQDs above 98 % with negligible effects. The effects of nine organic solvents (EA, DCM, DMF, MT, HEX, EAC, DMSO, EG, NBA) on the fluorescence of G-CQDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 13(C), the fluorescence of G-CQDs was significantly enhanced in DCM, DMF, EAC, and DMSO solvents, while the other solvents were reversed. This is caused by the different dispersion of G-CQDs in different solvent environments. In order to investigate the effect of solution polarity on the fluorescence performance of G-CQDs, ultrapure water and EA were mixed as solvents in different ratios (water: EA = 0 : 5, 1 : 4, 2 : 3, 3 : 2, 4 : 1, 5 : 0) to configure G-CQDs solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 13(D), the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs solution gradually increased with the increase of polarity, so it is better to use ultrapure water as the main solvent when preparing fluorescent ink.

The fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs varies with temperature as shown in Fig. 13(E). As shown in the figure, the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs basically increases (5 ∼ 20 °C) and remains stable within a certain temperature range (20 ∼ 40 °C), then decreases (40 ∼ 90 °C) with the increase of temperature. This is mainly because the agglomerations of G-CQDs nanoparticles are intensified with the increase or decrease of temperature which reduces the effective concentration, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. However, the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs remained above 80 % throughout the temperature range, indicating that G-CQDs has good thermal stability.

The patterns were drawn on the filter paper with a cotton swab dipped in the configured fluorescent ink, and dried and then placed under daylight and 365 nm UV light for photographing and recording. As shown in Fig. 14 , there is almost no trace in daylight and the green patterns under 365 nm UV light, which indicates that G-CQDs have great potential for application in information anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the practicality of G-CQDs in detecting real water samples of tap water from the laboratory. The tap water was centrifuged, filtered and mixed with different concentrations of Fe3+ and Co2+, respectively, and the content of Fe3+ and Co2+ in the real water samples was determined and calculated using the standard curve method. As shown in Table 3 , the spike recovery of Fe3+ ranged from 98.11 % to 106.80 % and Co2+ ranged from 95.87 % to 104.49 %, which indicates that the results of the test are accurate and reliable, and verifies the potential of G-CQDs for the detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in real samples.

In summary, green fluorescent G-CQDs were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal method, and a dual-responsive fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and Co2+ was developed based on the fluorescence “on-off” mechanism. This can be inferred from the series of changes produced by the addition of L-Cys to the G-CQDs-Fe3+ and G-CQDs-Co2+ quench systems: the fluorescence quench of G-CQDs by Fe3+ is due to the effect of intramolecular dynamics, in which the electrons of G-CQDs occupy the empty orbitals in the outer layer of Fe3+ to increase the number of positive charges, resulting in the fluorescence “off”; while the fluorescence quench of G-CQDs by Co2+ is static, which directly complexes with the functional groups on the surface of G-CQDs and thus blocks the luminescence channel. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in complex environments was achieved by two different quench mechanisms with a detection limit of 0.84 μM for Fe3+ and 0.95 μM for Co2+. Configuring it into fluorescent ink showed greater potential in information anti-counterfeiting, and it was used for the determination of Fe3+ and Co2+ in tap water with good spike recovery.
Zixuan Pu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qian Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Kuan Li: Data curation. Wenlin Fan: Formal analysis. Mingtian Li: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can in appropriately influence our work，there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and company.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Innovation Fund of Postgraduate, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering (No. Y2023004 and CX2023011), for which all the authors are grateful.
